results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Chinese Cinnamon (Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae)",
  "original_text": "WHO Monograph Plant: Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae. Common name: Chinese cinnamon. Latin name: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J. Presl (syn. Cinnamomum aromaticum Nees). Family: Lauraceae. Drug: The dried bark of the trunk and branches of Cinnamomum cassia. Major chemical constituents include: trans-cinnamaldehyde (55–90% of essential oil), cinnamyl acetate, cinnamyl alcohol, coumarin (0.45–2%), eugenol, cinnamic acid, procyanidins (oligomeric type-A and type-B), diterpenes (cinnzeylanol, cinnzeylanone), polyphenols, and tannins. Essential oil content: not less than 1.2% (v/w) per European Pharmacopoeia standards. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4.",
  "translation": "DEFINITION: Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae consists of the dried inner bark of the trunk and branches of Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J. Presl (Family: Lauraceae), stripped of its outer cork and dried. It is distinguished from Ceylon cinnamon (Cortex Cinnamomi, from C. verum J. Presl) by its thicker bark, darker reddish-brown colour, rougher texture, and notably higher coumarin content.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Cinnamomum cassia is an evergreen tree reaching 10–15 metres in height. Leaves are alternate or nearly opposite, ovate-oblong, 8–16 cm long, with three prominent veins arising from near the base (triplinerved). Leaf surfaces are glabrous above, pale and slightly pubescent below. Flowers are small, yellowish-white, borne in panicles. Fruit is an oval drupe, dark purple when ripe, approximately 1 cm long, enclosed at the base by a persistent perianth tube. Bark is thick, hard, and strongly aromatic, reddish-brown on the inner surface, greyish-brown and rough on the outer surface. Unlike C. verum, the bark does not form quills but is traded as flat or curved pieces (cassia sticks).

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to southern China (principally Guangxi, Guangdong, and Yunnan provinces) and northern Viet
results._label: WHO Monographs — Chinese cinnamon (Cortex Cinnamomi Cassiae)

  "original_text": "Stigma Croci — Crocus sativus L. (Iridaceae). The dried stigmas and tops of the styles of Crocus sativus L. Primary chemical constituents include: crocin (a water-soluble carotenoid digentiobioside ester, 6–16% dry weight), picrocrocin (a monoterpene glycoside responsible for bitter taste, ~4%), safranal (a volatile monoterpene aldehyde forming ~70% of the essential oil fraction, derived from picrocrocin degradation), and zeaxanthin (a carotenoid). Additional constituents include kaempferol, quercetin, and various flavonoids. The ISO standard (ISO 3632) grades saffron by crocin absorbance (≥190 units for Grade I), safranal content (20–50 units), and picrocrocin bitterness (≥70 units). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4.",
  "translation": "DEFINITION: Stigma Croci consists of the dried stigmas and the uppermost portions of the styles of Crocus sativus L. (family Iridaceae), commonly known as saffron. The pharmaceutical drug material comprises the thread-like, deep red-orange, dried stigmatic branches harvested by hand from the flower.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Crocus sativus is a sterile triploid perennial herb propagated exclusively by corm division. It grows 10–30 cm in height, producing 6–9 linear, grass-like leaves with a white, membranous midrib. The solitary flower is pale lilac to violet with distinctive purple veining; the three bright crimson-red stigmas are elongated (25–30 mm), funnel-shaped at their apex, and slightly serrated. The plant does not produce viable seed and is entirely dependent on human cultivation for reproduction.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Crocus sativus is believed to have originated in the Eastern Mediterranean and Southwest Asia (likely derived from the wild progenitor Crocus cartwrightianus). Primary commercial cultivation occurs in Iran (accounting for approximately 90% of global production), Spain (La Mancha regio

  "original_text": "Gummi-resina Gugguli — the dried oleogum resin obtained from the stem of Commiphora wightii (Arnott) Bhandari (Burseraceae), also known as Commiphora mukul (Hook. ex Stocks) Engl. Active constituents include guggulsterones (Z- and E-isomers, principally Z-guggulsterone and E-guggulsterone), guggulsterols (I–V), diterpenoids (mukulol, cembrene-A), ferulic acid, and polysaccharides.   "translation": "DEFINITION: Guggul resin (Gummi-resina Gugguli) is the air-dried oleogum resin tapped from incisions in the bark of the small thorny tree Commiphora wightii. The resin exudes naturally or after deliberate wounding and solidifies on exposure to air. The pharmaceutical preparation known as 'guggulipid' is a standardised ethyl acetate extract enriched to a defined guggulsterone content. The crude resin is yellowish-brown to reddish-brown, with a characteristic balsamic odour and bitter taste.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Commiphora wightii is a small, deciduous, multi-branched thorny shrub or tree reaching 1–4 m in height. Bark is papery, ash-coloured to yellowish-grey, peeling in thin flakes to reveal a greenish underlayer. Branches terminate in sharp spines. Leaves are small, 1–3 trifoliate, appearing seasonally; leaflets obovate, 1–5 cm, with crenate margins. Flowers are small, reddish-brown to pink, dioecious (male and female on separate plants), appearing before or with leaves. Fruit is a drupe, ovoid, 6–8 mm, red when ripe with a 2–4 valved stone.

results.original_text: Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae — the dried calyces and epicalyces of Hibiscus sabdariffa L. (Malvaceae). Principal chemical constituents include: anthocyanins [hibiscin / delphinidin-3-sambubioside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside, 1.5–2.5% dry weight], organic acids [citric acid, malic acid, tartaric acid, hibiscic acid], polysaccharides, flavonoids [quercetin, luteolin, gossypetin], phytosterols [beta-sitosterol], protocatechuic acid, hydroxycitric acid lactone [hibiscus acid], and mucilaginous polysaccharides. Anthocyanin content is the primary marker of pharmacological activity and commercial quality. Standardisation is typically against total anthocyanin content measured by pH differential spectrophotometry.
results.translation:
Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) calyces are the fleshy, deep-red, dried flower-cup structures harvested after the petals fall. The plant is an erect annual or perennial herb in the mallow family, growing 1–3 metres tall with toothed, palmate leaves and pale yellow flowers bearing a distinctive dark eye. It is native to tropical West Africa and South Asia and is now widely cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions globally — including Mexico, Egypt, Sudan, Thailand, China, and Jamaica — both as a beverage crop and a medicinal plant.

--- CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS ---
The dominant bioactive compounds are anthocyanins (principally delphinidin-3-sambubioside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside), which give the calyx its characteristic crimson colour and account for most antioxidant, antihypertensive, and antimicrobial activity. Organic acids — especially hibiscus acid (hydroxycitric acid lactone) and citric acid — contribute to the diuretic and lipid-modulating effects. Gossypetin and quercetin are the primary flavonoid aglycones. Protocatechuic acid (a phenolic metabolite of anthocyanin degradation) has demonstrated vasorelaxant and endothelium-protective activity in isolated vessel preparations.

--- PHARMACOLOGICAL MECHANISMS ---
• Antihypertensive: Inhibition of angiotensin-converting enzyme [ACE] activity; direct vasodilatory effect on vascular smooth muscle (calcium-channel antagonism); endothelium-dependent relaxation mediated by nitric oxide (NO) release. Animal studies (rat aorta ring assays) confirm protocatechuic acid and anthocyanins are the primary vasoactive agents.
• Diuretic: Organic acid load increases urinary volume and electrolyte excretion in rodent models; human studies show modest but significant natriuresis at standard tea doses.
• Hypolipidaemic: Inhibition of hepatic cholesterol biosynthesis and upregulation of LDL-receptor expression; hibiscus acid has structural similarity to hydroxycitric acid (the putative lipase inhibitor in Garcinia cambogia), but direct lipase inhibition in Hibiscus has not been robustly confirmed in humans.
• Antioxidant: High oxygen radical absorbance capacity [ORAC] values; free-radical scavenging by anthocyanins and protocatechuic acid reduces lipid peroxidation markers in vitro and in animal models.
• Antimicrobial: Aqueous and ethanolic calyx extracts show in vitro inhibitory activity against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Candida albicans, consistent with organic acid-mediated pH reduction and membrane disruption.

--- CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Key Trials) ---
1. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE EFFECT (Herrera-Arellano et al., 2004 — Mexico): Randomised, double-blind, controlled trial (n=75) comparing Hibiscus sabdariffa aqueous extract [standardised to 9.6 mg anthocyanins per dose, three times daily] versus captopril 25 mg twice daily in patients with mild-to-moderate hypertension (Stage I–II). After 4 weeks, the Hibiscus group showed mean systolic blood pressure reduction of 11.58 mmHg and diastolic reduction of 3.11 mmHg, compared to 13.68 mmHg systolic and 6.43 mmHg diastolic in the captopril group. Difference was not statistically significant, suggesting comparable efficacy for mild hypertension. Tolerability was superior in the Hibiscus group.
2. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE EFFECT (McKay et al., 2010 — USA, Tufts University): Randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial (n=65) in pre-hypertensive and mildly hypertensive adults. Three cups per day of brewed Hibiscus tea (standardised preparation) for 6 weeks produced a mean systolic blood pressure reduction of 7.2 mmHg (p=0.030) versus 1.3 mmHg for placebo. Effect was most pronounced in participants with higher baseline systolic pressure. No significant adverse events reported.
3. LIPID-LOWERING EFFECT (Mozaffari-Khosravi et al., 2009 — Iran): Randomised controlled trial (n=60) in patients with Type 2 diabetes. Hibiscus sabdariffa tea (two cups daily for 30 days) significantly reduced total cholesterol (mean −3.8%), LDL cholesterol (−8.0%), and triglycerides vs. black tea comparator, while HDL cholesterol increased. Renal and liver function tests remained within normal limits.

--- MEDICINAL USES (Traditional / Pharmacopoeial) ---
Widely used in traditional medicine systems across Africa, the Caribbean, Mexico, and South Asia for: hypertension, fever reduction (antipyretic tisane), liver complaints, urinary tract infections [UTI], constipation, and as a topical antimicrobial agent for wounds. Listed in the pharmacopoeias of Mexico (Farmacopea Herbolaria), Egypt (Egyptian Pharmacopoeia), and is referenced in African traditional medicine compendiums. Consumed as the beverage 'agua de Jamaica' (Mexico), 'karkadé' (Egypt/Sudan), 'bissap' (West Africa), and 'sorrel' (Caribbean).

--- ADVERSE REACTIONS ---
• Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Mild gastrointestinal effects (nausea, flatulence) reported at high doses.
• Potential additive hypotensive effect when combined with antihypertensive drugs — blood pressure monitoring advised.
• High organic acid content may worsen gastro-oesophageal reflux.
• Case reports suggest possible hepatotoxicity at very high doses in animal models; human hepatotoxicity not established at standard doses but warrants caution.
• Drug interaction: May reduce bioavailability of chloroquine (antimalarial) — concurrent use not recommended. Possible interaction with diuretics (potentiated effect).
• Weak oestrogenic activity reported in some in vitro assays; clinical relevance uncertain.

--- CONTRAINDICATIONS ---
• Pregnancy: Uterotonic activity demonstrated in animal studies; use during pregnancy is contraindicated.
• Lactation: Insufficient safety data; avoid.
• Concurrent chloroquine therapy: pharmacokinetic interaction documented.
• Known hypersensitivity to Malvaceae family plants.

--- WHO-RECOMMENDED ADULT DOSAGE ---
• Infusion (tea): 1.5–3 g of dried calyces in 150–250 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily.
• Standardised extract: Equivalent to approximately 9.6–14.4 mg total anthocyanins per dose, 2–3 times daily.
• Duration: Clinical trials support use for 4–6 weeks for antihypertensive indication; long-term safety beyond 6 weeks not fully established in controlled settings.
results.preparation: Infusion (aqueous tea): 1.5–3 g dried calyces per 150–250 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, 2–3 times daily. Standardised extract capsules: 9.6–14.4 mg total anthocyanins per dose, 2–3 times daily. Syrup or concentrated aqueous extract formulations also in use regionally. Duration of use: 4–6 weeks per clinical trial data.
results.condition: Mild to moderate hypertension (Stage I); hyperlipidaemia / dyslipidaemia adjunct (particularly LDL and triglyceride reduction); diuresis; traditional: fever, urinary tract infections, liver complaints
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4 — Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried calyces of Hibiscus sabdariffa L. (Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical name refers specifically to the fleshy calyx and epicalyx, not the flower petals. Quality standardisation is based on total anthocyanin content.

entities_mentioned.name: Hibiscus sabdariffa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Roselle; also known commercially as 'hibiscus tea,' 'sorrel,' 'karkadé,' 'bissap,' 'agua de Jamaica'
entities_mentioned.notes: Annual/perennial herb in Malvaceae; not the same species as ornamental Hibiscus rosa-sinensis. Widely cultivated pantropically.

entities_mentioned.name: Delphinidin-3-sambubioside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary anthocyanin pigment in Hibiscus sabdariffa calyces
entities_mentioned.notes: Key marker compound for standardisation and antihypertensive / antioxidant activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyanidin-3-sambubioside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Secondary anthocyanin in Hibiscus sabdariffa calyces
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-primary anthocyanin with delphinidin-3-sambubioside; both degrade to protocatechuic acid, which is independently vasoactive.

entities_mentioned.name: Hibiscus acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroxycitric acid lactone; a unique organic acid constituent of Hibiscus sabdariffa
entities_mentioned.notes: Structurally related to hydroxycitric acid in Garcinia cambogia; contributes to diuretic and possible lipid-modulating effects. Not to be confused with malic or citric acid also present in the calyx.

entities_mentioned.name: Protocatechuic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid; phenolic metabolite of anthocyanin degradation
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated vasorelaxant and endothelium-protective activity; considered a key downstream mediator of the antihypertensive effect.

entities_mentioned.name: Gossypetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A flavonoid [flavonol] characteristic of Hibiscus and Gossypium species; 3,5,7,8,3',4'-hexahydroxyflavone
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity. Useful chemotaxonomic marker distinguishing Hibiscus sabdariffa from adulterant species.

entities_mentioned.name: Captopril
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: ACE inhibitor antihypertensive drug (pharmaceutical comparator in Herrera-Arellano et al. 2004 clinical trial)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as positive control in the key Mexican RCT; Hibiscus extract showed statistically comparable (non-inferior) blood pressure reduction to captopril 25 mg in mild-to-moderate hypertension.

entities_mentioned.name: Chloroquine
entities_mentioned.notes: Documented pharmacokinetic interaction: Hibiscus sabdariffa aqueous extract reduces oral bioavailability of chloroquine, likely via altered gastrointestinal absorption. Concurrent use is contraindicated.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE CONSTRUCTION NOTE: This entry was generated as a comprehensive Plantacopia corpus segment based on WHO Monograph structure for Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae (WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 4). The source provided was a structured prompt rather than raw OCR text, so no OCR corrections were necessary. Content is synthesised from established WHO monograph data and the cited primary clinical literature. 2. CLINICAL TRIAL CITATIONS: The three trials cited (Herrera-Arellano 2004, McKay/Tufts 2010, Mozaffari-Khosravi 2009) are real, peer-reviewed, published studies consistent with the WHO Vol. 4 monograph evidence base. Numerical data reported are consistent with published findings but should be verified against primary sources for formal citation. 3. ANTHOCYANIN STANDARDISATION: Total anthocyanin content varies substantially by cultivar, harvest time, and drying method. The 9.6 mg dose used in the Herrera-Arellano trial is the most cited standardised dose for antihypertensive indication and is the basis for the WHO dosage recommendation. 4. PREGNANCY CONTRAINDICATION: The uterotonic effect has been demonstrated in isolated uterine muscle preparations and whole-animal studies; it is considered a Class C/contraindicated risk in pregnancy in all major herbalism regulatory frameworks consistent with WHO guidance. 5. HEPATOTOXICITY SIGNAL: Animal-model hepatotoxicity at suprapharmacological doses has been reported but is not considered a clinical risk at WHO-recommended doses. Flagged here for completeness per WHO monograph adverse-reaction reporting standards. 6. COMMON NAME DISAMBIGUATION: 'Hibiscus tea' in commerce almost universally refers to Hibiscus sabdariffa calyces, not to any other Hibiscus species. However, 'roselle' is the more precise common name and is preferred in pharmacopoeial contexts to avoid confusion with ornamental species.
results._label: WHO Monographs — Roselle / Hibiscus (Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae)

  "title_inferred": "Hops (Strobulus Lupuli) — WHO Monograph Entry",
  "original_text": "Strobulus Lupuli (Humulus lupulus L., Cannabaceae). The dried female inflorescences (strobiles/cones) of Humulus lupulus, containing bitter acids (alpha-acids/humulones 2–12%; beta-acids/lupulones 2–10%), essential oil (0.3–1.0%; comprising myrcene, humulene, beta-caryophyllene, linalool, geraniol), prenylated flavonoids (principally 8-prenylnaringenin [8-PN] and isoxanthohumol), chalcones (xanthohumol), and methylbutenol (2-methyl-3-buten-2-ol) generated from alpha-acids during drying. Tannins (2–4%) and polyphenols also present. Official pharmacopoeial standard: minimum 0.35% volatile oil and defined bitter-value indices per European Pharmacopoeia.",
  "translation": "DEFINITION: Strobulus Lupuli consists of the dried, ripe female inflorescences (commonly called 'cones' or 'strobiles') of Humulus lupulus L. (family Cannabaceae), harvested at maturity and carefully dried. The pharmaceutical drug comprises the intact or lightly broken strobiles, including their glandular trichomes (lupulin glands), which concentrate the pharmacologically active resins and essential oil.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Humulus lupulus is a vigorous, perennial, dioecious climbing herb that can reach 6–8 metres in a single growing season. Stems (bines) are twining, rough, and angled. Leaves are opposite, broadly ovate to cordate, 3–5 lobed, serrate-margined, with prominent veins and rough surfaces. Female plants produce pendant, cone-like inflorescences (strobiles) composed of papery, overlapping bracts and bracteoles; at their base sit yellow lupulin glands containing resins and oil. Male flowers are borne in loose axillary panicles on separate plants. The plant dies back to the rootstock annually in temperate climates.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to temperate regions of Europe, western Asia, and North America. Widely cultivated commercially — principally in Germany (Hallertau), United Kingdo

  "original_text": "Folium Murrayae — Murraya koenigii (L.) Spreng. (Rutaceae). Fresh or dried leaves containing carbazole alkaloids (mahanimbine, girinimbine, koenimbine, mahanine, cyclomahanimbine), flavonoids (rutin, quercetin), essential oils (α-pinene, β-phellandrene, sabinene, linalool), phenolic compounds (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid), terpenoids (murrayanol, murrayanine), and significant quantities of vitamin A, B, C, and minerals including iron and calcium.   "translation": "DEFINITION: Folium Murrayae consists of the fresh or dried leaves of Murraya koenigii (L.) Spreng. (family Rutaceae), commonly known as curry leaf or sweet neem. The dried leaf should contain not less than 0.05% total carbazole alkaloids calculated as mahanimbine on a dry-weight basis.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Murraya koenigii is a small to medium-sized deciduous or semi-evergreen aromatic tree or shrub, typically reaching 4–6 metres in height. Leaves are pinnately compound (imparipinnate), 15–40 cm long, bearing 11–21 leaflets; leaflets are ovate-lanceolate, 2–4 cm long, asymmetric at the base, with finely serrate margins, dark glossy green on the adaxial surface, paler beneath, and strongly aromatic when crushed due to volatile oil glands. Flowers are small, white, fragrant, borne in terminal corymbs. Fruit is a small, ovoid, purple-black berry, 1–1.5 cm in diameter, containing a single seed. The bark is dark grey with corky lenticels.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to the Indian subcontinent (India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal) and extending through Southeast Asia into southern China. Widely cultivated throughout tropical and subtropical regions, including Africa, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, primarily as a culinary herb. Grows best in well-drained, slightly acidic

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Black Seed / Black Cumin (Nigella sativa)",
  "original_text": "Semen Nigellae — Nigella sativa L. (Ranunculaceae). The dried ripe seeds of Nigella sativa L. Major chemical constituents include: fixed oils (32–40%), principally composed of unsaturated fatty acids — linoleic acid (50–60%), oleic acid (20%), and palmitic acid (13%); volatile oil (0.4–2.5%) containing thymoquinone (TQ, 27–57%), thymohydroquinone, thymol, carvacrol, trans-anethole, and 4-terpineol; protein (26%); alkaloids including nigellicine and nigellidine; saponins including alpha-hederin; sterols; and tannins. Thymoquinone is regarded as the principal bioactive constituent responsible for the majority of pharmacological effects documented in preclinical and clinical studies.",
  "translation": "## PLANTACOPIA ENTRY — BLACK SEED / BLACK CUMIN

---

### 1. DEFINITION
Semen Nigellae consists of the dried, ripe seeds of *Nigella sativa* L. (family Ranunculaceae), commonly known as black seed, black cumin, or nigella. The seeds are small (1–3 mm), angular, black externally and white internally, and possess a characteristic aromatic, peppery odour. They are distinguished from caraway (*Carum carvi*) and true cumin (*Cuminum cyminum*), with which they are sometimes confused in vernacular usage. The material must comply with pharmacopoeial standards for moisture content, ash values, volatile oil content, and foreign matter.

---

### 2. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
*Nigella sativa* is an erect annual herb, 20–60 cm in height. Leaves are pinnately divided into linear segments, alternate, and pale green. Flowers are solitary, terminal, pale blue or white, with five petite nectary-bearing petals and numerous stamens. The fruit is an inflated capsule composed of 5–6 follicles united to the middle, containing numerous small, trigonous (three-angled), rough, black seeds. The plant is glabrous to sparsely hairy and flowers from May to July in its native range.

---

### 3. GEOGRAPHI

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Holy basil is an aromatic, erect, much-branched annual or short-lived perennial herb, 30–60 cm tall, with a characteristic clove-like fragrance. Stems are purplish or greenish, hairy (pubescent). Leaves are opposite, petiolate, ovate to elliptic (2–5 cm long), entire or serrate, often tinged purple, and densely glandular. Flowers are small, purplish-white, arranged in whorls on elongated terminal racemes. Calyx is bilabiate; corolla is two-lipped. Seeds are small, brownish, mucilaginous when wetted.

The rhizome is harvested, cleaned, and dried; it is intensely bitter in taste. This bitterness — a key organoleptic marker of quality — derives primarily from the iridoid glycoside fraction.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Picrorhiza kurroa is a small, hardy perennial herb (10–25 cm tall) with a creeping, fleshy rhizome and fibrous roots.   "translation": "DEFINITION: Cortex Terminaliae Arjunae is the dried stem bark of Terminalia arjuna (Roxb. ex DC.) Wight & Arn., a large deciduous tree of the family Combretaceae. The bark is collected from mature trees, dried, and used in powdered, decoction, or extract form. It constitutes a major drug in Ayurvedic medicine, where it is classified as a 'hridya' (cardiotonic) herb.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Terminalia arjuna is a large, deciduous tree reaching 20–25 metres in height, with a broadly spreading crown and drooping branches. The bark is smooth, grey-green to pinkish-grey externally, and pale red internally; it exfoliates in large flat pieces. Leaves are sub-opposite, oblong to elliptic (5–14 cm long), coriaceous, with a rounded or cordate base, and characteristically bear two glands near the base of the blade. Flowers are small, white or pale yellow, borne in axillary spikes or panicles. The fruit is a drupe, 2.5–5 cm long, with 5 prominent fibrous wings. The root system is extensive, typical of riverine habitat trees.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Terminalia arjuna is native to the Indian subcontinent, distributed widely across India — especially in the sub-Himalayan tract, Bihar, Orissa (Odisha), Madhya Pradesh, and along river banks and moist deciduous forests. It is also found in Sri Lanka, Myan

The stem is succulent with prominent warty, gray-brown bark and conspicuous aerial roots hanging from the branches. Leaves are cordate (heart-shaped), membranous, broadly ovate, 10–20 cm long, with a deeply cordate base and long petioles. Flowers are small, unisexual, greenish-yellow, appearing in racemes or panicles when the plant is leafless (March–June). Fruits are drupes, red to orange when ripe, borne in clusters. The plant is dioecious. The characteristic fleshy, rope-like stem — the source of the drug — is easily recognizable by its greenish-gray surface covered with succulent tubercles and by its abundant starchy transverse section.

Major chemical constituents include: steroidal saponins (principally protodioscin, protogracillin, dioscin, gracillin, and tribulosin; 1.0–3.0% total), flavonoids (kaempferol, kaempferol-3-glucoside, kaempferol-3-rutinoside, quercetin), alkaloids (harmane, norharmane, beta-carboline derivatives), phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, campesterol), fatty acids (linoleic acid, oleic acid), and trace minerals (K, Ca, Mg). BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Tribulus terrestris L. is a prostrate or ascending annual or perennial herb in the family Zygophyllaceae. Stems are branched, radiating from the root crown, 10–60 cm in length, covered with silky hairs. Leaves are opposite, paripinnate, 3–7 cm long, with 5–8 pairs of oblong-elliptic leaflets (6–15 mm × 2–5 mm), pubescent on both surfaces. Flowers are solitary, axillary, 4–10 mm in diameter, with five pale yellow petals. Fruit is a schizocarp splitting into 5 mericarps, each woody, with 2 long and 2 short rigid spines (the 'punctures' giving the common name); the spines are sharp enough to puncture bicycle tires and animal hooves. Seeds 3–5 per mericarp.

  "translation": "DEFINITION: Rhizoma Zingiberis is the fresh or dried rhizome (underground stem) of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae), commonly known as ginger. The dried rhizome must yield not less than 4.5 mL/kg of essential oil on a dried-drug basis.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Zingiber officinale is an erect perennial herb reaching 60–120 cm in height. The plant produces elongated, lance-shaped leaves (15–30 cm) arising from reed-like, sheathing stems. The true rhizome is irregularly branched, laterally compressed, and pale yellowish-buff externally with a pale yellow interior. Flowers are greenish-yellow with a purple lip and emerge on a separate spike directly from the rhizome; they are rarely produced in cultivation. The plant does not reproduce by seed under typical agricultural conditions.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to Southeast Asia (believed to originate in India or China); now cultivated throughout tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, with major production in India, China, Nigeria, Jamaica, Australia, and Fiji. India remains the world's largest producer. The plant requires a warm, humid
